summary: Ancient Greek philosopher (341-270 BCE) who founded Epicureanism, teaching at 'the Garden' in Athens. Argued that pleasure (especially the absence of pain) is the highest good. Key doctrines: atomism with the clinamen (swerve), ataraxia and aponia, the tetrapharmakos, the problem of evil, and three criteria of truth. His follower Lucretius wrote De Rerum Natura. Pierre Gassendi revived his thought in the early modern period.

works.name: Atomism, the Swerve, and Physics
works.description: Epicurus's physics derived mostly from Democritus but introduced key innovations. He formulated the concept of a necessary void making motion possible and argued for the material nature of the soul. His follower Lucretius proposed that atoms spontaneously undergo clinamen (swerve), creating randomness and free will. Epicurus conjectured that gods occupy areas devoid of atoms called metakosmia. He used the example of a square tower appearing round to argue all sense perception is true (though judgment can err). Unlike Democritus, Epicurus believed atoms possess weight. Stephen Greenblatt's The Swerve discusses the rediscovery of Lucretius's poem.

clues.clue: Clinamen/swerve: atoms deviate, creating free will; Lucretius expanded on this in De Rerum Natura
examples: Lucretius borrowed this philosopher's concept of the 'swerve' to explain the possibility of free will

clues.clue: Physics derived from Democritus; believed atoms possess weight (unlike Democritus)
examples: this philosopher's view of nature was mostly derived from Democritus of Abdera

clues.clue: Necessary void makes motion possible; material nature of the soul
examples: formulated the concept of a necessary void which makes motion possible, and argued for the material nature of the soul

clues.clue: Gods occupy metakosmia (atom-free spaces); no concern for mortals
examples: conjectured that the gods occupied an area devoid of atoms called the metakosmia

clues.clue: Square tower appearing round: all sense perception is true, judgment can err
examples: introduced the common example of a round tower appearing square in his argument that all sense perception is true

clues.clue: Stephen Greenblatt's The Swerve about rediscovery of Lucretius
examples: A work inspired by the philosophy of this non-Roman thinker is the principal subject of Stephen Greenblatt's book The Swerve

clues.clue: Hermann Usener published massive compilation of works from this philosopher's school
examples: Hermann Usener published a massive compilation of works inspired by this philosopher's school

works.name: Ataraxia, Aponia, and Ethics
works.description: Epicurus argued that the goal of life is eudaimonia, achieved through ataraxia (freedom from distress) and aponia (freedom from pain). He differentiated happiness from mere pleasure. The tetrapharmakos ('fourfold remedy') argues against fearing gods and death, that what is good is easy to get, and what is terrible easy to endure. His argument against fearing death: 'when we are, death is not come, and when death is come, we are not.' He divided pleasure into 'static' and 'kinetic' types. He suggested eating simple foods because those accustomed to luxury are likely to lose it. His school dominated over that of Aristippus of Cyrene.

clues.clue: Ataraxia (freedom from distress) and aponia (freedom from pain) as life's goals
examples: argued that the perfect life was characterized by freedom from distress and freedom from pain, known as ataraxia and aponia

clues.clue: Tetrapharmakos (fourfold remedy): don't fear gods, death, what is good is easy, terrible is easy to endure
examples: proposed a 'four fold remedy' which argues that fearing death is irrational

clues.clue: 'When we are, death is not come; when death is come, we are not' (Letter to Menoeceus)
examples: 'when we are, death is not come, and when death is come, we are not'

clues.clue: Divided pleasure into 'static' and 'kinetic' types
examples: divided one concept in his own philosophy into 'static' and 'kinetic' types

clues.clue: School dominated over Aristippus's Cyrenaics
examples: founded a philosophical school that ultimately dominated over one established by Aristippus of Cyrene

clues.clue: Pleasure is the highest good; but prudence is the most precious virtue
examples: the instruction that prudence is the most precious of all virtues

clues.clue: 'Empty sounds' do not refer to real being; focusing on them is the main source of pain
examples: claimed that the fear of death and the desire for immortality have this quality

works.name: The Garden, Letters, and Community
works.description: Epicurus taught at 'the Garden,' his school in Athens whose gate inscription reportedly read 'Stranger, here you will do well to tarry.' He enjoyed a simple diet of barley bread and water. His surviving letters (to Herodotus, Menoeceus, and Pythocles) are preserved in Book X of Diogenes Laertius's Lives. The Letter to Herodotus summarizes physics including atomism and three criteria of truth (aisthesis, pathe, prolepseis). The Letter to Menoeceus contains ethical teachings. Followers celebrated Eikas on the twentieth of each month. Pierre Gassendi's commentaries led an early modern revival.

clues.clue: The Garden: school in Athens; gate: 'Stranger, here you will do well to tarry'
examples: taught at a school called 'the Garden'

clues.clue: Letters to Herodotus, Menoeceus, Pythocles preserved in Diogenes Laertius Book X
examples: expounded his beliefs about happiness and metaphysics in letters to Herodotus, Pythocles, and Menoeceus

clues.clue: Three criteria of truth: aisthesis (sense), pathe (feelings), prolepseis (preconceptions)
examples: established feelings, preconceptions, and sense-perceptions as three criteria of truth

clues.clue: Simple diet of barley bread and water
examples: enjoyed a diet of barley bread and water

clues.clue: Pierre Gassendi: early modern revival; commentaries on Epicurus; rejected Aristotelian necessity
examples: The aforementioned works by Gassendi were commentaries on the thoughts of this Greek thinker

clues.clue: On Nature: Epicurus's major work; Lucretius titled De Rerum Natura after it
examples: On Nature served as the foundation for Lucretius' poem On the Nature of Things

clues.clue: Works preserved by Mt. Vesuvius eruption (Herculaneum)
examples: many of whose works are very literally preserved thanks to Mount Vesuvius's eruption

works.name: Problem of Evil and Reception
works.description: Epicurus is credited with the problem of evil (his 'namesake paradox'), asking why God allows suffering if omnipotent and benevolent. David Hume popularized this as a trilemma in Dialogues Concerning Natural Religion. Sextus Empiricus argued against Epicurus's idea that everything perceptible is true. Epicureans were compared to the Indian Carvaka school. Tolstoy discussed Epicureanism in A Confession.

clues.clue: Problem of evil / Epicurean paradox: popularized in Hume's Dialogues Concerning Natural Religion
examples: credited with introducing the problem of evil in David Hume's Dialogues Concerning Natural Religion
often considered the first person to state the problem of evil, which is his namesake paradox

clues.clue: Sextus Empiricus argued against Epicurus's 'everything perceptible is true'
examples: Sextus Empiricus' Against the Logicians contains an extended argument against this thinker's idea

clues.clue: Compared to Indian Carvaka school (hedonistic, atomistic)
examples: The Carvakas have been compared to this Greek thinker's lineage

comprehensive_summary:
Epicurus was an ancient Greek philosopher (341-270 BCE) who founded one of the major Hellenistic philosophical schools. He taught at 'the Garden' in Athens, living simply on barley bread and water. His physics derived from Democritus's atomism but introduced the crucial concept of clinamen (swerve), in which atoms spontaneously deviate, creating randomness and free will. His follower Lucretius expanded on this in the six-book poem De Rerum Natura.

Epicurus's ethical philosophy centered on achieving eudaimonia through ataraxia (freedom from distress) and aponia (freedom from pain). He argued that pleasure is the highest good but distinguished it from raw sensual pleasure, dividing pleasure into 'static' and 'kinetic' types. The tetrapharmakos, or fourfold remedy, instructed followers not to fear gods or death. His famous argument against fearing death was: 'when we are, death is not come, and when death is come, we are not.' He taught that prudence is the most precious virtue, and that 'empty sounds' not referring to real being are the main source of pain.

Epicurus established three criteria of truth: aisthesis (sense-perception), pathe (feelings), and prolepseis (preconceptions). He used the example of a square tower appearing round to argue that all sense perception is true, though judgment can err. He conjectured that gods occupy metakosmia (atom-free spaces) and have no concern for mortals.

His surviving letters to Herodotus, Menoeceus, and Pythocles are preserved in Book X of Diogenes Laertius. He is credited with the problem of evil, popularized by Hume. His school dominated over Aristippus's Cyrenaics. Pierre Gassendi led an early modern revival. Stephen Greenblatt's The Swerve discusses the rediscovery of Lucretius.
recursive_suggestions: De Rerum Natura / Lucretius (heavily clued poem)

Philosopher: divided pleasure into 'static' and 'kinetic' types; school dominated over Aristippus of Cyrene's hedonistic school
Philosopher: surviving letters to Herodotus, Menoeceus, and Pythocles preserved in Book X of Diogenes Laertius; wrote On Nature
Philosopher: established three criteria of truth: aisthesis (sense), pathe (feelings), and prolepseis (preconceptions)
Philosopher: works 'very literally preserved' thanks to Mount Vesuvius's eruption at Herculaneum
Philosopher: credited with stating the problem of evil, a trilemma popularized in David Hume's Dialogues Concerning Natural Religion
Philosopher: Sextus Empiricus's Against the Logicians contains an extended argument against this thinker's view that everything perceptible is true
